title: Pseudocholinesterase deficiency (succinylcholine)
vignette: Patient given succinylcholine (and propofol) before surgery has prolonged paralysis for hours postoperatively. Underlying cause?
whatMatters: Succinylcholine is cleared by plasma pseudocholinesterase; hereditary deficiency (BCHE polymorphism) -> hours of paralysis.
strategy: Match the drug to its inactivation route. Succinylcholine/mivacurium -> plasma pseudocholinesterase (not liver/kidney/redistribution).

eliminations.correctIf: Drug was an aminoglycoside or vancomycin (renally cleared, renal failure prolongs effect)

eliminations.why: Propofol is a hypnotic, doesn't paralyze.
eliminations.correctIf: Drug was propofol (anesthetic hypnotic; offset by redistribution, not metabolism)

eliminations.why: No liver disease; wouldn't last hours.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had severe liver failure AND received a drug metabolized by the liver (not plasma cholinesterase)
Myasthenic patient was RESISTANT to succinylcholine (not prolonged paralysis — opposite scenario)

reasoning: Normally cleared by plasma pseudocholinesterase (<10 min)
Hereditary deficiency (BCHE) -> drug lingers at NMJ
mechanism: Pseudocholinesterase (butyrylcholinesterase, BCHE; autosomal recessive) normally degrades ~90% of succinylcholine. Deficiency -> more reaches the NMJ -> prolonged paralysis (hours). Check dibucaine number. Supportive: sedate + ventilate.
memoryHook: Succinylcholine won't quit = pseudocholinesterase can't split.
